advantages: Produces a full merge hierarchy (dendrogram), so you can explore multiple values of k from one run without re-fitting.
No need to specify k in advance—useful when the natural number of groups is unknown or debated.
Linkage options and dendrogram cuts give interpretable, nested structure for exploratory analysis and visualization.
Deterministic given a fixed distance matrix and linkage rule (unlike random-init k-means), aiding reproducible EDA.
disadvantages: Naive agglomerative implementations are O(n³) time and O(n²) memory—impractical for very large datasets.
Once a merge happens, it cannot be undone; early mistakes propagate through the entire tree.
Sensitive to distance metric, feature scaling, and linkage choice—different settings can yield very different trees.
Choosing the cut height (k) is subjective without external validation metrics or domain constraints.
